jokes.text: Wow, that's weird. / Ooh, dollar for a stapler, that's pretty good.
jokes.setup: Dwight discovers his office supplies mysteriously in a vending machine
jokes.punchline: Jim's mock surprise and commentary on the 'great deal' for the stapler
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim prank executed with perfect deadpan delivery. The visual of office supplies in a vending machine is inherently absurd, and Jim's faux-innocent commentary adds layers.

jokes.text: I know you did this 'cause you're friends with the vending machine guy. / Who, Steve? / Yeah, Steve.
jokes.setup: Dwight accuses Jim of conspiracy with vending machine operator
jokes.punchline: Jim casually confirms knowing the vending machine guy by name
jokes.explanation: Builds the prank's mythology while showing Jim's preparation. The casual 'Steve' detail makes it feel lived-in.

jokes.text: My wallet? Um, oh, there it is. J1.
jokes.setup: The prank extends beyond office supplies to personal items
jokes.punchline: Dwight's wallet is also in the vending machine, with a specific location code
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - just when you think you understand the scope of the prank, it gets bigger. The location code 'J1' is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Here you know what, you can have some nickels. 5, 10. 15, 20, 25...
jokes.setup: Jim offers to help Dwight buy back his own wallet
jokes.punchline: The prolonged counting of nickels emphasizes the absurd indignity
jokes.explanation: The slow nickel counting extends the humiliation. Less elegant than the setup but serves the cringe comedy.

jokes.text: A ski mask and a swimsuit? So that he can have us rob a bank, and then escape through the sewers. And brush our teeth.
jokes.setup: Michael's mysterious memo requests seemingly unrelated items
jokes.punchline: Jim's logical but ridiculous crime scenario, capped with the mundane toothbrush detail
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating logic that builds to the perfect mundane deflation. Classic Jim observational humor with perfect structure.

jokes.text: Stanley...bo banley. / Banana fana fo fanley. / Me mi mo manley. / Stanley.
jokes.setup: Stanley tries to get serious information about the event
jokes.punchline: Michael responds with the children's song game, then says Stanley's name normally
jokes.explanation: Pure Michael Scott avoiding responsibility through childish deflection. The normal 'Stanley' at the end suggests he ran out of steam.

jokes.text: Should my wife tell her boss she's not coming in tomorrow? / Maybe, I don't know. / Not maybe, yes or no. / Well, no, but... okay, don't spoil it for everybody.
jokes.setup: Stanley demands concrete information for practical planning
jokes.punchline: Michael treats legitimate workplace coordination as 'spoiling' his surprise
jokes.explanation: Perfect character clash - Stanley's practical needs vs Michael's theatrical priorities. Shows Michael's obliviousness to real consequences.

jokes.text: In January? / It's cheaper.
jokes.setup: Someone questions the timing of a lake cruise in winter
jokes.punchline: Michael's matter-of-fact admission that cost trumped comfort
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - two words reveal Michael's priorities and explain the absurd timing. The deadpan delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I have a test for business school tomorrow night. Is it okay if I skip the cruise and study for that? / No, this is mandatory. But don't worry, you know what? You're going to learn plenty. This is going to turn your life around, Ryan. / I'm already in business school.
jokes.setup: Ryan prioritizes actual business education over team event
jokes.punchline: Michael promises life-changing education to someone literally already in business school
jokes.explanation: Michael's obliviousness to Ryan's actual qualifications. The irony of promising education to the most educated person there.

jokes.text: Why did you tell us to bring a bathing suit? / To throw you off the scent. / Yeah, but I bought a bathing suit. / Well, just keep the tags on and you can return it. / I took the tags off already. / Well, that's not my fault, okay? Just-we're not going to pay for a bathing suit.
jokes.setup: Michael's misdirection causes real financial consequence for employee
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate deflection of responsibility and refusal to reimburse
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael avoiding accountability. The escalation from 'keep tags on' to 'not my fault' shows his selfishness.

jokes.text: I attended a Tony Robbins event by the airport last year, and... It wasn't the actual course. You have to pay for the actual course. But, it talked about the actual course.
jokes.setup: Michael claims motivational speaker credentials
jokes.punchline: Admits he only attended a promotional preview, not the actual training
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael Scott - inflating minor experience into expertise, then accidentally revealing the truth. The progression of admissions is beautifully structured.

jokes.text: Leader...ship. The word ship is hidden inside the word leadership. As its derivation.
jokes.setup: Michael attempts profound linguistic insight
jokes.punchline: His pseudo-intellectual etymology is completely wrong
jokes.explanation: Michael's confident wrongness is character-perfect. The dramatic pause and false etymology attempt shows his pseudo-intellectual pretensions.

jokes.text: Last year, Michael's theme was 'bowl over the competition.' So, guess where we went?
jokes.setup: Jim references Michael's previous literal interpretation of metaphors
jokes.punchline: Implies they went bowling based on the theme
jokes.explanation: Establishes Michael's history of taking business metaphors literally. Good setup for the current ship metaphor sequence.

jokes.text: Let me just explain. I see the sales department as the furnace. / A furnace? / Yeesh, how old is this ship?
jokes.setup: Michael uses an antiquated ship analogy
jokes.punchline: Employee points out the technology dates the metaphor
jokes.explanation: Smart observation that Michael's business metaphors are as outdated as his references. Quick and effective.

jokes.text: I mean who saw the movie 'Titanic'? / Not really sure what movie you're talking about. / You sure you got the title right? / 'Titanic'? / I think you're thinking of 'The Hunt for Red October'.
jokes.setup: Michael references the most famous movie ever made
jokes.punchline: Employees pretend not to know Titanic while suggesting an obscure submarine movie
jokes.explanation: Brilliant group gaslighting of Michael. The suggestion of Hunt for Red October shows they know exactly what they're doing.

jokes.text: Michael stands in the front of the boat and says he's king of the world within the first hour, or I give you my next paycheck.
jokes.setup: Jim predicts Michael's behavior based on character knowledge
jokes.punchline: Specific confident bet shows how predictable Michael is
jokes.explanation: Sets up a payoff that will come later. Shows Jim's deep understanding of Michael's predictable theatricality.

jokes.text: Michael, everyone in the engine room drowned. / No, thank you, spoiler alert.
jokes.setup: Employee points out flaw in Michael's Titanic metaphor
jokes.punchline: Michael treats historical fact as a movie spoiler
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael logic - avoiding uncomfortable truths by treating them as spoilers. The 'no thank you' is beautifully dismissive.

jokes.text: They're happy down there in the furnace room. And they're dirty and grimy and sweaty. And they're singing their ethnic songs.
jokes.setup: Michael romanticizes manual labor
jokes.punchline: Casually problematic 'ethnic songs' comment reveals his unconscious bias
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael casual insensitivity. The progression from romantic to problematic is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Actually, that might be warehouse. / What? / The, no, no. No I didn't... okay, well, the...
jokes.setup: Michael realizes he may have said something problematic
jokes.punchline: His flustered non-denial and inability to recover
jokes.explanation: Michael's panic when he realizes he may have crossed a line. The stuttering retreat shows his awareness without real apology.

jokes.text: Aye, aye, Captain. / A three hour tour. A three hour tour.
jokes.setup: Michael's ship metaphor continues
jokes.punchline: Dwight references Gilligan's Island theme song
jokes.explanation: Standard TV reference humor. Works for Dwight's character but not particularly original or clever.

jokes.text: Pam, you are Mary Ann. We have... The Professor and Ginger. Welcome aboard. Angela, you are Mrs. Howell. Lovey.
jokes.setup: Michael assigns Gilligan's Island roles to coworkers
jokes.punchline: The casting reveals how he sees his colleagues
jokes.explanation: Reveals Michael's perception of office dynamics through TV archetypes. The Angela-as-Lovey casting is particularly apt.

jokes.text: I am the Skipper, and Dwight, you will be Gilligan. / Cool. / Actually, I'm the Skipper. But you can be Gilligan. / Oh, I'd rather die.
jokes.setup: Michael assigns roles, Dwight accepts enthusiastically
jokes.punchline: When Michael changes his mind, Dwight's reaction swings to dramatic rejection
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael-Dwight dynamic. Dwight's extreme swing from enthusiasm to disgust is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: In an office, when you are ranking people, manager is higher than captain. On a boat, who knows? It's nebul-ose.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to assert authority over the boat captain
jokes.punchline: 'Nebul-ose' - a made-up word trying to sound sophisticated
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael malapropism. 'Nebul-ose' sounds like he's combining 'nebulous' with something Latin. Shows his insecurity about authority.

jokes.text: Hey look. I'm king of the world!
jokes.setup: Jim's earlier prediction about Michael's behavior
jokes.punchline: Michael does exactly what Jim predicted he would do
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to Jim's setup. The predictability IS the joke - Michael is exactly as transparent as Jim knew he'd be.

jokes.text: Not only am I your ship's captain, I am also your party captain! / And I'm your party captain too!
jokes.setup: Captain Jack announces his dual role
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately copies and competes with the announcement
jokes.explanation: Shows Michael's competitive nature and inability to let others have the spotlight. The immediate mimicry is painfully character-accurate.

jokes.text: If the boat's a-rocking, don't come knocking. / Michael. / What?
jokes.setup: Michael tries to be the fun party guy
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate sexual reference that gets shut down by the captain
jokes.explanation: Michael's inappropriate comment and obliviousness to the captain's disapproval. Standard Michael cringe behavior.

jokes.text: You guys, it's like we're in high school and we're at the cool table.
jokes.setup: Michael wants to feel like part of the group
jokes.punchline: His desperate need to be seen as 'cool' comes across as pathetic
jokes.explanation: Michael's insecurity about social status. The high school reference reveals he never emotionally graduated.

jokes.text: No, she was total little Miss Artsy Fartsy in high school. She wore the turtleneck and everything.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Pam's high school persona
jokes.punchline: Roy's dismissive characterization reveals his lack of appreciation for Pam
jokes.explanation: Shows Roy's casual dismissiveness toward Pam's interests. 'Artsy Fartsy' reveals his anti-intellectual attitude.

jokes.text: Piss slop who cares-a?
jokes.setup: Pam mentions Bishop O'Hara High School
jokes.punchline: Michael's juvenile rhyming insult of the school name
jokes.explanation: Juvenile wordplay typical of Michael's arrested development. Gets a mild laugh but lacks sophistication.

jokes.text: A-W-E... S-O-M-E, awesome, awesome is what we are we're the football superstars. / We crushed you like 42 to 10.
jokes.setup: Michael performs what he claims to remember from high school
jokes.punchline: The cheer is clearly made up on the spot, plus the specific score claim
jokes.explanation: Michael's obvious improvisation and competitive boasting. The awkward cheer and specific score show his need to 'win' the conversation.

jokes.text: Not like you 'New Yawkers.'
jokes.setup: Michael is being welcoming to the corporate visitor
jokes.punchline: His attempt at New York accent reveals provincial prejudices
jokes.explanation: Standard regional stereotype humor. Shows Michael's small-town mindset but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Not now, Mike, we're doing the limbo! / Ha, ha, that's right, partiers. It's time to limbo, limbo, limbo!
jokes.setup: Michael tries to start his presentation
jokes.punchline: Captain Jack shuts him down, Michael immediately abandons his plan to join the party
jokes.explanation: Shows Michael's lack of authority and his immediate flip to following rather than leading. The 'limbo, limbo, limbo' is weak but in character.

jokes.text: I need a volunteer to come up here and hold my stick. / Me. / Usually it's a woman. / I'm stronger.
jokes.setup: Captain needs help with limbo stick
jokes.punchline: Dwight's aggressive volunteering and masculine justification for a simple task
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight overcompensation and need to prove his superiority even in trivial situations.

jokes.text: I was the youngest pilot in Pan Am history. When I was four, the pilot let me ride in the cockpit, and fly the plane with him. And I was four and I was great! And I would have landed it, but my dad wanted us to go back to our seats.
jokes.setup: Michael offers Dwight a chance to 'steer the ship'
jokes.punchline: Dwight's completely fabricated childhood aviation story with escalating absurdity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Dwight fabrication with perfect escalation. The progression from 'youngest pilot' to 'I was four' to 'would have landed it' is expertly absurd.

jokes.text: It is a primal art form used in ancient times to express yourself with the body. And communicate.
jokes.setup: Dance contest begins
jokes.punchline: Michael's pseudo-intellectual commentary on dancing
jokes.explanation: Michael's tendency to over-intellectualize simple activities. The pompous tone contrasts with the silly dance context.

jokes.text: Sometimes you have to take a break from being the kind of boss that's always trying to teach people things. Sometimes you just have to be the boss of dancing.
jokes.setup: Michael participates in the dance contest
jokes.punchline: His profound declaration about being 'the boss of dancing'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael pseudo-wisdom. 'Boss of dancing' is an absurd concept delivered with complete sincerity. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Hey, come inside and talk to me. / I can't. Do you want us to run aground woman?
jokes.setup: Pam asks Dwight to come inside
jokes.punchline: Dwight takes his fake steering responsibility seriously with dramatic language
jokes.explanation: Dwight's commitment to the fake responsibility is perfect. 'Run aground woman' has nautical authority mixed with casual sexism.

jokes.text: Snorkel shot! Who's next? Come on, Pammy, come on, come on! / I'm not doing that.
jokes.setup: Party activities escalate to drinking games
jokes.punchline: Pam's firm refusal contrasts with the rowdy atmosphere
jokes.explanation: Shows Pam's boundaries and dignity amid the chaos. The contrast works but isn't particularly memorable.

jokes.text: Hey, why don't we find like, a quieter place to hang out? / You know what, I've just gotta wait for Darryl to do his shot. Just a minute. Come on, Darryl, Darryl!
jokes.setup: Jim tries to get alone time with Pam
jokes.punchline: Pam prioritizes watching Darryl's drinking over Jim's romantic gesture
jokes.explanation: Jim's romantic gesture being deflected by party atmosphere. Shows Pam's current loyalty to Roy and the group.

jokes.text: Sometimes, I just don't get Roy.
jokes.setup: Roy ignores Pam's desire for quiet conversation
jokes.punchline: Jim's massive understatement of his frustration with Roy
jokes.explanation: Perfect understatement hiding deeper feelings. The camera-friendly delivery suggests Jim's internal monologue.

jokes.text: So. What's it like dating a cheerleader?
jokes.setup: Long pause after Jim's observation about Roy
jokes.punchline: Jim's attempt at casual conversation that reveals his deeper interest
jokes.explanation: The awkward transition and loaded question reveal Jim's romantic interest while maintaining plausible deniability.

jokes.text: So, what's this presentation all about? / It is about...priorities. And making decisions, using the boat as an analogy. What is important to you? If the boat is sinking, what do you save? / Women and children. / No, no. Salesmen and profit centers.
jokes.setup: Michael presents his business philosophy through disaster scenario
jokes.punchline: Michael rejects human decency in favor of business priorities
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy revealing Michael's warped priorities. The contrast between moral and business thinking is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: That's a stupid analogy. / O-kay, well obviously you don't know anything about leadership.
jokes.setup: Actual ship captain criticizes Michael's nautical metaphor
jokes.punchline: Michael dismisses the expertise of a real captain
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Michael lecturing an actual ship captain about boats. His obliviousness to the contradiction is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Well, I was the captain of a PC-1 Cyclone Coastal Patrol Boat during Desert Storm. / Wow, you should be the motivational speaker.
jokes.setup: Captain Jack reveals actual military command experience
jokes.punchline: Michael's sarcastic response shows he can't acknowledge being outclassed
jokes.explanation: Escalates the authority conflict. Michael's sarcasm reveals his inability to concede superior experience.

jokes.text: I'd like to be engaged. / How did you manage to pull that off?
jokes.setup: Ryan shares personal aspiration in business context
jokes.punchline: Michael treats engagement like a business achievement requiring strategy
jokes.explanation: Michael's inability to separate business and personal contexts. The 'pull that off' phrasing treats love like a business deal.

jokes.text: Suppose your office building's on fire. Jim, who would you save? / Let's see... the customer. Cause the customer is king. / Not what I was looking for, but a good thought. / He's just sucking up.
jokes.setup: Michael poses hypothetical crisis scenario to Jim
jokes.punchline: Jim gives business-speak answer instead of the personal one Michael wants; Ryan calls out the sucking up
jokes.explanation: Jim deflects Michael's leading question with corporate speak, and Ryan's observation adds another layer. Good character interaction.

jokes.text: You know what, I would save the receptionist. I just wanted to clear that up.
jokes.setup: Michael's earlier question was leading toward a specific answer
jokes.punchline: His transparent attempt to publicly declare feelings for Pam
jokes.explanation: Michael's clumsy attempt at romantic gesture. The 'clear that up' makes it sound like correcting a factual error.

jokes.text: Pam, um, I think enough is enough. I think we should set a date for our wedding. How about June 10th? Come on, let's do it.
jokes.setup: Romantic tension building throughout episode
jokes.punchline: Roy's sudden proposal comes at worst possible timing and manner
jokes.explanation: Ironic that Roy's romantic gesture comes right after Michael and Jim's declarations. The casual 'let's do it' undermines the romance.

jokes.text: Come on, I don't want to take credit for this, but Roy and I were just having a conversation about making commitments and making choices, right? Did I motivate you?
jokes.setup: Roy proposes to Pam
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately tries to take credit while claiming he doesn't want credit
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael contradiction - claiming not to want credit while desperately seeking it. The transparent motivation is perfect.

jokes.text: No, it was, it was Captain Jack. / Well.... / Captain Jack! / Could have been either one of us because, pretty much, we were saying the same thing.
jokes.setup: Roy credits Captain Jack instead of Michael
jokes.punchline: Michael's desperate attempt to share credit even when explicitly contradicted
jokes.explanation: Michael's inability to accept that he didn't contribute to the moment. His rationalization is pathetically transparent.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, hey, hey, I got an idea, I got an idea. I can marry you right now as captain of the ship, huh? I can marry you as Regional Manager of Dunder Mifflin!
jokes.setup: Michael wants to be part of the romantic moment
jokes.punchline: His offer to perform the wedding with made-up authority
jokes.explanation: Michael's desperate grab for relevance. The dual authority claim shows his confusion about what gives him power.

jokes.text: No, no, no, no, I want my mom and dad to be there. / Then, I'll give you away! / No, thank you.
jokes.setup: Pam politely declines Michael's wedding offer
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately offers another inappropriate role, gets firmly rejected
jokes.explanation: Michael's inability to read social cues. Pam's firm 'No, thank you' shuts down his boundary-crossing.

jokes.text: Do you think that'll ever be us? / No. / What is wrong with you? Why did you even bring me here tonight? / I don't know. / Let's break up.
jokes.setup: Karen asks about future marriage to Jim
jokes.punchline: Jim's blunt 'No' triggers immediate relationship termination
jokes.explanation: Brutal economy and escalation. Jim's honesty immediately destroys the relationship. The rapid progression from question to breakup is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Seasick? / Captain Jack says you should look at the moon. / Captain Jack's a fart face.
jokes.setup: Jim offers seasickness advice
jokes.punchline: Michael's childish insult reveals his hurt feelings
jokes.explanation: 'Fart face' is juvenile but perfectly captures Michael's emotional regression when threatened by authority.

jokes.text: I'm on medication. / Really? What? / Vomicillin.
jokes.setup: Jim asks about Michael's medication
jokes.punchline: Michael's made-up medication name combining 'vomit' and penicillin
jokes.explanation: Clever portmanteau that sounds almost plausible. Classic Michael fake expertise with a creative linguistic twist.

jokes.text: The ship, is sinking. Okay? We're going down, right now. Just wrap your heads around the reality of that.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to regain control with his leadership exercise
jokes.punchline: His dramatic declaration of disaster on a party cruise
jokes.explanation: Michael's desperate attempt to create a teaching moment with inappropriate drama. The serious tone contrasts with the party setting.

jokes.text: Captain Jack is gone. In five minutes, this ship is going to be at the bottom of the lake. And there aren't enough spaces on the lifeboats.
jokes.setup: Michael's sinking ship scenario continues
jokes.punchline: He eliminates the actual captain from his fantasy disaster
jokes.explanation: Michael conveniently removes his authority rival from the scenario. The dramatic buildup maintains tension despite absurdity.

jokes.text: I'm in the brig, see? Boat's not as corporate friendly as advertised.
jokes.setup: Michael has been confined by the captain
jokes.punchline: His casual complaint about the boat's business accommodations
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael response to consequences - treating imprisonment as a customer service issue. The 'corporate friendly' phrasing is brilliant.

jokes.text: If he had just waited and heard what I had to say, he would be motivated right now and not all wet.
jokes.setup: Someone apparently jumped overboard during Michael's presentation
jokes.punchline: Michael blames the person for missing his motivational message
jokes.explanation: Michael's obliviousness to cause and effect. He sees the panic response as missing out on wisdom rather than his fault.

jokes.text: What happened to you? / Captain Jack has a problem with authority.
jokes.setup: Jim finds Michael confined
jokes.punchline: Michael blames the captain for having authority issues
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - Michael, who clearly has authority issues, projects this onto the captain. The psychological projection is expertly executed.

jokes.text: Well, it's nice for you. Your friend got engaged. / She was always engaged. / Roy said the first one didn't count.
jokes.setup: Michael mentions Pam's engagement as good news for Jim
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact correction reveals the long engagement's lack of progress
jokes.explanation: Reveals the hollow nature of Roy's gesture. Jim's deadpan delivery emphasizes the lack of real change.

jokes.text: You know, to tell you the truth, I... used to have a big thing for Pam. So... / Really? You're kidding me. You and Pam? I would have never... put you two together. You really hid it well, God!
jokes.setup: Jim confesses his feelings for Pam
jokes.punchline: Michael's complete obliviousness to what was obvious to everyone else
jokes.explanation: Michael's obliviousness to office dynamics is character-perfect. His amazement at Jim 'hiding it well' when it was completely obvious.

jokes.text: You know, I made out with Jan. / Yeah, I know.
jokes.setup: Michael shares what he thinks is secret information
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact acknowledgment that everyone knows
jokes.explanation: Michael thinks he's sharing shocking news but everyone already knows. Jim's casual response deflates Michael's drama.

jokes.text: Well, if you like her so much, uh, don't give up. She's engaged. / B.F.D. Engaged ain't married. Never, ever, ever give up.
jokes.setup: Jim explains why he can't pursue Pam
jokes.punchline: Michael's inappropriate encouragement with increasing intensity
jokes.explanation: Michael's terrible advice delivered with motivational speaker intensity. 'B.F.D.' and the repetitive 'never' show his commitment to bad guidance.

jokes.text: Don't worry, Michael. I'm taking us to shore. / It's a fake wheel, dummy.
jokes.setup: Dwight reassures Michael about his steering competence
jokes.punchline: Captain reveals Dwight has been steering with a decorative wheel
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to earlier setup. Dwight's confident navigation was always meaningless. The 'dummy' is brutal but earned. Excellent physical comedy premise.
